title: Nutrition during illness
summary: There is no special diet that cures a cold or influenza. Eat according to appetite, prioritising fluids, easy-to-tolerate foods, protein and overall nutritional adequacy.
evidenceSnapshot: Evidence for chicken soup or individual foods changing disease duration is weak. Adequate nutrition matters most when intake is poor or deficiency exists.

interventions.name: Normal balanced food as tolerated
interventions.finding: Supports energy, protein and micronutrient needs without requiring a special protocol.
interventions.practical: Use small meals: soup, oats, toast, rice, fruit, vegetables, eggs, legumes or other tolerated protein.
interventions.mechanism: Provides substrates for immune and tissue function.
interventions.cautions: Adjust for allergies, swallowing problems and medical diets.

interventions.name: Chicken or vegetable soup
interventions.finding: Provides fluid, salt, energy and comfort; limited laboratory or small human studies do not prove a clinical cure.
interventions.practical: Choose a nourishing soup with vegetables and protein.
interventions.mechanism: Warmth, hydration and aroma may improve comfort.

interventions.name: Protein-rich foods
interventions.finding: Helpful when illness reduces intake, particularly for older or frail people.
interventions.practical: Include a tolerated protein source at meals or snacks.
interventions.mechanism: Supports muscle maintenance and immune proteins.

interventions.name: Alcohol
interventions.finding: Can worsen sleep, dehydration, reflux and medicine side effects.
interventions.practical: Skip alcohol while significantly unwell or taking sedating/interactive medicines.
interventions.cautions: Increases risk with paracetamol misuse and sedatives.

practicalSteps: Prioritise drinking if appetite is low for a day or two.
Use bland, soft foods for sore throat or nausea.
Return to normal balanced meals as appetite returns.
Do not pressure a child to eat; offer familiar foods and monitor fluids/urine.
avoidOrCare: Prolonged poor intake is more concerning in infants, older adults and people with diabetes.
Seek advice if swallowing is unsafe or vomiting prevents fluids.

myths.claim: Starve a fever, feed a cold.
myths.reality: There is no evidence-based reason to deliberately fast either illness.

myths.claim: Dairy must be avoided because it makes mucus.
myths.reality: Dairy may change mouthfeel but is not shown to increase respiratory mucus.
Garlic is food, not a proven antiviral treatment for an established cold.

redFlags: Unintentional weight loss or poor intake lasting several days in a vulnerable person
unsettled: The clinical relevance of anti-inflammatory effects observed in laboratory studies of soup ingredients is unknown.
